posts.name: Todd French
posts.cooked: George Hejna was over last night and encouraged me to open one of my 2008 Cathiard Vosne-Romanee and it was tremendous. I do NOT have the Burgundy bug yet, and that’s a very good thing - I have seen what happens, and the amount of money that can be spent at the blink of an eye. I loved - LOVED - the Cathiard, however. The first red Burgundy I can recall with a very noticeable flint component to it, as well as just being a quality wine texturally, and perfect red fruit presence. I hasten to ask, but wonder if you might be willing to point me in the right direction for similarly-priced (similarly priced at the time, which was $50!), similarly-styled producers?

posts.name: Paul Jaouen
posts.cooked: Cathiard makes special wines. Some other Vosne-Romanee producers I like in no particular order are Grivot, LaMarche, Hudelot-Noellat, and Felettig. Of those, Felettig probably has more wines in your price range then others although I think the basic Grivot is also well-priced.

posts.name: R Nanda
posts.cooked:
In your price range, Coquard Loison-Fleurot is another producer to look for. Similar stylistically to Cathiard – i.e., bit more extraction, plushness of fruit and new oak.

Too bad that Cathiard village Vosne is now $100+

posts.name: John J
posts.cooked:
Where are you currently getting Sylvain Cathiad for $50 bucks a bottle? Thats an incredible price

I would add Bachelet and Mugneret Gibourg to the mix abiet a little more expensive, but comparable in quality.

I would add Bachelet and Mugneret Gibourg to the mix abiet a little more expensive, but comparable in quality.

Thanks, all.

The Cathiard at $50 was in 2011…

posts.cooked: Try Gerard Mugneret , you will love it .

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: I’m fond of mongard mugernet and confuron cotetidot

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked: I really like what Charles has been doing at Arnoux Lachaux since he took over, particularly the reduction in new oak. The village vosne and nuits are still (relatively) affordable outside of 2015. The higher end wines are expensive but worth it in my opinion.

posts.name: R Nanda
posts.cooked:
A.Gillette:

I really like what Charles has been doing at Arnoux Lachaux since he took over, particularly the reduction in new oak. The village vosne and nuits are still (relatively) affordable outside of 2015. The higher end wines are expensive but worth it in my opinion.

Envoyer just offered 15s at very attractive price points.

I’m a big fan of Arnoux, though under Charles, I think Arnoux is stylistically more refined than Cathiard.

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked:
Todd. You started with one of the very best.

Lots of choices.

Ghislaine Barthod
Mugneret-Gibourg
Hudelot- Noellat

I love Grivot and Felletig as Paul noted. If you want under the radar, check out Heitz-Lochardet.

If you want some good negociant wine, nobody is making better wine than Erwan Faiveley.
I love Jadot too for domaine properties.

At $50 you are going to have to really do some selecting.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: In and around that price range I’m also pretty big fan of the Marchand Tawse range; they have a lot of nice village wine in that range and some premier crus for not much more.

posts.name: R@y.Tupp@+sch
posts.cooked: None of Mugneret-Gibourg’s wines are anywhere close to $50 these days.

Faiveley Mercurey Clos des Myglands and Clos du Roy hit both of these (actually Domaine, I think).

posts.name: Joel Galang
posts.cooked:
Have had a number of Cathiards from Vosne village, Close de L’ Orme, Nuits Aux Thorey, Nuit Murgers, Suchots and Malconsorts. Someone even generously opened a RSV for us one time. These wines are very pricey now, which is sad (I bought Aux Thorey for $80) and IMHO, it is very hard to get something today at $50 that is in the same style and quality as your $50 2008 Vosne village. The primary culprit is that Burgundy in general has gotten really expensive in the last 2 vintages (15 and 16). So even wines like Barthod Chambolle village is way above $50 (even for 2014).

Anyway, I would like to throw in Meo-Camuzet as a producer with a style that is slightly similar to Cathiard. Its the really high quality oak that they use that give them that ooomph and bot IMHO have a slight modern tendency. IMHO I think Cathiard wines are more modern style of Burgundy yet still pull off the terrior and finesse needed to keep them from going over the top. My favorite of all Cathiards when you consider bang for the buck is Murgers. If money is no object, Malconsorts is definitely the best wine they make IMHO. The RSV is good, but at that price I’d rather add a couple hundred bucks and go for DRC RSV.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
It’s quite difficult to think of producers that resemble Cathiard, as the style is pretty singular. What’s more, a lot has changed there between 2018 and 2008. The wines still see a lot of new oak but I think they have more concentration to back it up. I think Sebastian takes more risks too - in 2017 he was one of the last to harvest. He spends a lot of time working in the vines.

FWIW, I’m sitting at a table blind tasting Corton-Charlemagne with a bunch of other critics and some of the biggest wine buyers in the UK trade, and no one can think of anything that resembles Cathiard.

Of course, if you want to list oaky producers in Vosne, the list is very long.

Several producers mentioned here don’t have anything in common with Cathiard, at least to my palate.

posts.name: CHIU LIN
posts.cooked:
J. Galang:

My favorite of all Cathiards when you consider bang for the buck is Murgers. If money is no object, Malconsorts is definitely the best wine they make IMHO. The RSV is good, but at that price I’d rather add a couple hundred bucks and go for DRC RSV.

Just like you, my go-to wine in Cathiard is the Murgers and also my largest holdings from the producer, the 2nd favorite wine is the L’Orme, it rivals a lot of 1er Crus. I opened an ‘07 Murgers in ‘07 Burgundy tasting recently, it was able to hang in there with much bigger names.

And I agree with Kelley that Cathiard is rather unique but I love it. Maybe Roty is the closest in style.

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

It’s quite difficult to think of producers that resemble Cathiard, as the style is pretty singular. What’s more, a lot has changed there between 2018 and 2008. The wines still see a lot of new oak but I think they have more concentration to back it up. I think Sebastian takes more risks too - in 2017 he was one of the last to harvest. He spends a lot of time working in the vines.

FWIW, I’m sitting at a table blind tasting Corton-Charlemagne with a bunch of other critics and some of the biggest wine buyers in the UK trade, and no one can think of anything that resembles Cathiard.

Of course, if you want to list oaky producers in Vosne, the list is very long.

Several producers mentioned here don’t have anything in common with Cathiard, at least to my palate.

Todd needs to branch out. I don’t think sticking to Vosne or trying to find an oaky style is what he is describing. He just needs to drink some good wine from Burgundy IMHO. He also wants a certain price point that is going to be almost impossible and drink good producers where purity of fruit and silky texture is important. Hopefully the parameters can be more elastic for him so that he can be successful. I think trying to chase styles seems like a way to end up rejecting the whole lot.

fancy_title: Burgundy by Sylvain Cathiard - what else do you recommend?
